content.text: Model selection is the single highest-leverage decision in a GenAI system's carbon footprint. A frontier model can emit
50–400× more CO₂e per token

than a well-chosen smaller model on the same task — before any other optimisation is considered. An energy-efficient model selection policy translates this arithmetic into a governance process: it defines when engineers must consider efficiency alongside capability, what evidence is required to justify a more energy-intensive choice, and how approved model selections age and are revisited as the landscape evolves.

attrs.alt: Model Selection Decision Tree showing three paths (small, mid-size, frontier) with efficiency levers and policy gates panels

content.text: Why Model Size Is the Dominant Variable

Inference energy scales roughly with the number of active parameters: a 70B-parameter model consumes approximately 8–10× the GPU flops of a 7B model for an equivalent prompt. The energy gap widens further because large models require more memory bandwidth, preventing the tight batching that amortises fixed GPU overhead across many concurrent requests.
Empirical data from published model efficiency benchmarks (MLPerf inference results, research from Hugging Face and EleutherAI) illustrates the spread:
Model Family
Approx. Parameters
Relative CO₂e / 1M tokens
Suitable Task Complexity
GPT-3.5 / Mistral-7B class
1× (baseline)
Classification, summarisation, simple Q&A, RAG retrieval ranking
Llama-3-70B / Claude Haiku class
Code generation, multi-step reasoning, structured extraction
GPT-4 / Claude claude-sonnet-4-6 class
~1T+ (MoE or dense)
Novel reasoning, multi-domain synthesis, safety-critical decisions
Key insight:

These ratios are approximate and model-version-dependent. Measure empirically for your actual workload using codecarbon or a cloud carbon dashboard. The ratio varies significantly between batch inference (high GPU utilisation, low overhead per token) and online inference (moderate utilisation, higher per-token overhead).
The Model Selection Policy Framework

The policy establishes three tiers of model size, each with different governance requirements for approval and use. The tiers are not fixed to specific models — they are anchored to measured efficiency scores so the policy remains valid as models improve.
Tier 1 — Preferred (≤ 20B parameters or equivalent efficiency)
Self-service: any engineer may use Tier 1 models without pre-approval.
Models in this tier must be benchmarked for the task category at onboarding.
Examples: Mistral-7B, Llama-3-8B, Phi-3-mini, GPT-3.5-turbo, Claude Haiku.
Carbon budget: no per-project cap — Tier 1 models are presumptively compliant.
Tier 2 — Justified Use (20B–100B parameters or equivalent)
Requires manager approval and a written justification: 'We benchmarked Mistral-7B at 74% accuracy on our eval set; Llama-3-70B achieves 89%; the 15pp gap is material to the user experience because…'
Must document the benchmark methodology: eval set, accuracy metric, sample size.
Carbon budget: Tier 2 models consume the per-BU monthly carbon allocation at 8× the rate of Tier 1. Budget exhaustion suspends Tier 2 access for the remainder of the month.
Examples: Llama-3-70B, Mixtral-8x22B, Claude claude-sonnet-4-6, GPT-4o-mini.
Tier 3 — Frontier (>100B effective parameters, or any closed API model priced >$0.01/1K tokens)
Requires Director/VP approval plus a detailed justification referencing Tier 1 and Tier 2 benchmarks.
Must include a sunset clause: Tier 3 approval expires in 6 months and triggers an automatic review cycle.
Carbon budget: Tier 3 models consume the carbon allocation at 100× Tier 1. Monthly cap enforced hard — requests beyond the cap return a governance error, not a model response.
Examples: GPT-4, Claude Opus, Gemini Ultra, large proprietary enterprise LLMs.
Model Efficiency Scoring
The policy requires each approved model to carry a measured efficiency score. This score is the primary data point for tier classification and budget consumption tracking.
Efficiency Optimisation Techniques

Model selection is a first-order decision, but within a given model there are complementary techniques that substantially reduce per-token energy consumption. These are not optional nice-to-haves — the policy requires that teams justify their absence for Tier 2 and Tier 3 deployments.
Quantisation

Quantisation reduces weight precision from float32 or bfloat16 to int8 or int4. The energy reduction comes from both reduced memory bandwidth (the dominant bottleneck for large model inference) and from enabling higher batch sizes per GPU. The accuracy impact at 8-bit quantisation is typically under 1% on standard benchmarks; 4-bit quantisation (GPTQ, AWQ, GGUF) trades 1–3% accuracy for a 2–4× throughput improvement.

# Serve a quantised model with HuggingFace + bitsandbytes
from transformers import AutoModelForCausalLM, AutoTokenizer, BitsAndBytesConfig
import torch

quantisation_config = BitsAndBytesConfig(
    load_in_4bit=True,
    bnb_4bit_use_double_quant=True,
    bnb_4bit_quant_type="nf4",
    bnb_4bit_compute_dtype=torch.bfloat16,
)

model = AutoModelForCausalLM.from_pretrained(
    "meta-llama/Meta-Llama-3-70B-Instruct",
    quantization_config=quantisation_config,
    device_map="auto",
)
# Model memory footprint: ~35 GB (4-bit) vs ~140 GB (bfloat16)
# Enables serving on 2× A100 80GB instead of 4× A100 80GB
Prompt Compression

Long contexts are the main driver of per-request energy cost: attention computation scales as O(n²) with context length. Prompt compression libraries (LLMLingua, SelективFusion, RECOMP) use a small 'compressor' model to remove tokens that are redundant for the downstream task, typically achieving 3–20× compression ratios on retrieval-augmented contexts with under 5% accuracy degradation.

# LLMLingua prompt compression (pip install llmlingua)
from llmlingua import PromptCompressor

compressor = PromptCompressor(
    model_name="microsoft/llmlingua-2-bert-base-multilingual-cased-meetingbank",
    use_llmlingua2=True,
    device_map="cpu",             # Compressor can run on CPU; cheap
)

long_context = """... 8000-token RAG retrieved passages ...""".strip()
instruction   = "Summarise the key financial risks discussed."

result = compressor.compress_prompt(
    context=[long_context],
    instruction=instruction,
    target_token=1000,           # Compress to ~1000 tokens
    rank_method="longllmlingua",
)

compressed_prompt = result["compressed_prompt"]
ratio             = result["ratio"]              # e.g. 0.12 = 12% of original
print(f"Compressed to {ratio:.0%} of original length ({result['origin_tokens']} → {result['compressed_tokens']} tokens)")
# Feed compressed_prompt to the target LLM
Semantic Response Caching

Identical or near-identical prompts are common in enterprise GenAI applications: FAQ bots, code-completion assistants, and summarisation pipelines often receive the same logical request expressed in slightly different words. Semantic caching stores embeddings of previous prompts and returns the cached response when cosine similarity exceeds a threshold, eliminating the inference call entirely.

# GPTCache for semantic caching (pip install gptcache)
from gptcache import cache
from gptcache.adapter import openai
from gptcache.embedding import Onnx
from gptcache.manager import CacheBase, VectorBase, get_data_manager
from gptcache.similarity_evaluation.distance import SearchDistanceEvaluation

# Configure cache with semantic similarity
onnx = Onnx()
data_manager = get_data_manager(
    CacheBase("sqlite"),
    VectorBase("faiss", dimension=onnx.dimension),
)
cache.init(
    embedding_func=onnx.to_embeddings,
    data_manager=data_manager,
    similarity_evaluation=SearchDistanceEvaluation(),
    similarity_threshold=0.85,   # 0.85 cosine similarity → treat as cache hit
)
cache.set_openai_key()

# openai.ChatCompletion.create() now goes through cache automatically
response = openai.ChatCompletion.create(
    model="gpt-4",
    messages=[{"role": "user", "content": "Summarise the Q3 earnings report."}],
)
# Second identical request returns cached response in <1ms with zero inference cost
Region Selection for Carbon Optimisation

Cloud region selection is a deployment decision with a direct carbon impact: running the same GPU workload on a low-carbon grid (Nordic hydro, nuclear-heavy US regions) vs a high-carbon grid (US East coal/gas mix) produces a 10–20× difference in Scope 2 emissions, without any code change.
Region (Cloud)
Grid Carbon Intensity (g CO₂e/kWh)
CFE %
Relative Scope 2 (vs US-East)
europe-north1 (GCP Finland)
northeurope (Azure Norway)
eu-west-1 (AWS Ireland)
us-east-1 (AWS N. Virginia)
1.0× (baseline)
us-central1 (GCP Iowa)
ap-southeast-1 (AWS Singapore)
* GCP Iowa's coal-heavy grid is partially offset by renewable energy purchases; market-based accounting lowers the effective rate.

from dataclasses import dataclass

# Grid carbon intensity (g CO₂e/kWh) — update quarterly from electricitymaps.com
GRID_INTENSITY: dict[str, float] = {
    "europe-north1": 17,
    "northeurope":   24,
    "eu-west-1":    233,
    "eu-central-1": 311,
    "us-east-1":    415,
    "us-central1":  461,
    "us-west-2":    120,   # Oregon — hydro mix
    "ap-northeast-1": 462, # Tokyo
}

def optimal_training_region(
    available_regions: list[str],
    latency_constraint_ms: float | None = None,
) -> str:
    """Return the lowest-carbon region from the available set.
    Ignores regions that would violate data residency constraints
    (caller must pre-filter available_regions for compliance).
    """
    candidates = [r for r in available_regions if r in GRID_INTENSITY]
    if not candidates:
        raise ValueError("No regions with known carbon intensity found in available_regions")
    return min(candidates, key=lambda r: GRID_INTENSITY[r])

# Usage: filter to GDPR-compliant regions, then pick lowest carbon
gdpr_compliant = ["eu-west-1", "eu-central-1", "europe-north1", "northeurope"]
best = optimal_training_region(gdpr_compliant)
print(f"Optimal GDPR-compliant training region: {best}")
# → europe-north1 (17 g CO₂e/kWh)
Carbon Budget Enforcement

A model selection policy without budget enforcement is advisory. The enforcement mechanism translates the tier multipliers into a concrete monthly carbon allowance per business unit, tracked in real time and enforced at the API gateway or model proxy layer.
Summary
Energy-efficient model selection is a governance discipline, not just an engineering optimisation. Key takeaways:
Tier classification is the primary control.
Smaller models are presumptively compliant; larger models require written justification backed by benchmark evidence. This inverts the common default of reaching for the most capable model first.
Efficiency levers compound.

Quantisation (2–4×), prompt compression (3–20×), and semantic caching (0–90% hit rate) can reduce effective carbon cost by an order of magnitude on top of model size reduction. Document their absence in Tier 2/3 approval forms.
Region selection is a free win.

Running on a low-carbon grid costs the same as a high-carbon grid in cloud pricing but emits 10–20× less CO₂e. For training runs, always prefer nordic or hydro-heavy regions when data residency rules permit.
Budget enforcement makes the policy real.
Without hard limits, tiering becomes advisory. Enforce carbon budgets at the API gateway level; budget exhaustion must return a governance error, not a silent fallback to a cheaper model.
Sunset clauses prevent stale approvals.
A Tier 3 model approved in 2024 may have a Tier 1 equivalent available in 2025. Six-month expiry on frontier-model approvals drives systematic re-evaluation as the efficiency frontier advances.
